data.title: Xcel Energy: Clears A Major Hurdle, Still A Hold For Now
data.description:
Back in April, I recommended a cautious hold position on Xcel Energy Inc. (NASDAQ:XEL). My initial interest in the stock was due to its $45 billion capital plan across the next five years, aimed at meeting the growing energy demands from data centers and increasing its focus on renewable energy. However, the stock appeared expensive, particularly due to the uncertainty around the financial impact of the wildfire liabilities. This concern was recently addressed with a settlement of $640 million, a price tag much lower than many had predicted. As a direct result of this news, the stock increased by 7%, as it removes an uncertainty that has been a dark cloud for the company over the last few years.

On this news, I wanted to revisit my thesis. The good news is that Xcel is still focused on its five-year capital plan to push into renewable energy and grid modernization. In Q2 2025, the management also reconfirmed its guidance, despite feeling the pressure of rising costs. While the stock’s valuation looks more balanced compared to peers, it is not trading at a clear discount either. For investors entering now, I continue to recommend a Hold rating, as while there is upside from renewable growth and data center demand, I remain cautious of the ongoing cost pressures and the regulatory risks that still remain.

Company Updates

Xcel Energy is based in Minneapolis and provides 3.7 million customers with electricity and 2.1 million customers with natural gas services across eight US states. The company is in a transitional phase in which it is moving towards renewable energy, and it has a goal of being carbon-free by 2050.

The biggest recent update is the $640 million settlement of claims connected to the 2021 Marshall Fire in Colorado. The litigation connected to the wildfires has been a major issue for the company. Many believed that it would cost the company billions of dollars, as it was one of the most destructive wildfires in the state's history. Within this mindset, the news of the claims immediately benefited the stock price. We saw the stock price increase by 7% in a day, exactly because investors had feared much more financial damage. Importantly for potential investors, this helps clear uncertainty around future liabilities and allows management to re-focus on its growth projects. In the Q2 2025 earnings call, the management team also reconfirmed FY2025 EPS guidance as indicated in the image below.

It should be noted that Xcel missed its Q1 EPS expectations by $0.11, although Q2 2025 resulted in a beat, as shown in the table below. The company has missed four out of the last five quarters, so there is some uncertainty as to whether it can meet its guidance.

Q2 2025 Financial Performance

Xcel’s Q2 2025 results showed us that the company can still grow, even though it’s spending heavily on new projects. Profit per share came in at $0.75, which is an increase from $0.54 in the same quarter last year. If we look at the FH 2025, the profit also increased YoY from $1.42 in 2024 to $1.59. Revenue grew to $3.29 billion, which is an increase of nearly 9% YoY. However, expenses are increasing, and there have been growing costs connected to things like the company's interest payments on debt, maintenance costs, and depreciation. But overall, Xcel is proving it can handle its plan to invest heavily thanks to a strong and growing demand and some more favorable regulatory benefits. However, it is uncertain if these benefits will remain under the current political environment.

Valuation

As in my previous article, I have compared Xcel to NextEra Energy (NEE), Duke Energy (DUK), and The Southern Company (SO). The valuation is relatively on par with its peers. Xcel has a forward price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio of 20.4. This is just above that of Duke Energy, which has a forward P/E of 19.52. Its forward PEG ratio of 2.56 is lower than all the other peers, which indicates that investors are paying less for growth compared to the others. Overall, across the Seeking Alpha Quant valuation metrics, the company appears to be fairly valued. It is no longer at a premium, but it is not a cheap stock either. Investors should take into consideration the company's growth potential, but also the risk of its five year capital heavy investment strategy.

Risks

Although recent news has been well-received by investors, Xcel still faces risks that should be taken into consideration. Firstly, not the entire wildfire litigation cloud has passed. This is because the Smokehouse Creek litigation and other claims are not fully resolved. Furthermore, there is regulatory uncertainty, which is another important risk. There has been growing uncertainty and unpredictability around tariffs and benefits that are to be received from environmental efforts through taxes. The company is also investing in large-scale projects that can greatly benefit the company in the long run, but it will be dependent on management's ability to execute and continue to generate profits and cash flow. Finally, due to Xcel's high debt intake, it is sensitive to interest rates. These could impact the company's financial position in the long run.

Final Thoughts

Xcel Energy’s wildfire settlement has taken a big weight off the stock, and the market immediately jumped onto the news, increasing the stock price significantly in one day. Xcel is also at the start of an exciting five-year capital investment plan that could benefit from the growing data center demand and aims to get the company to carbon-zero by 2050. However, the stock is still not cheap, and there is a lot of uncertainty around increasing costs and regulatory changes under a new political regime. Therefore, for now, I maintain a Hold. I believe the stock is worth watching, but I think it is best to wait for a better entry point or once there is more weight behind the growth plans.

data.title: Slowdown Signals: Are Leading Indicators Flashing Red?
data.description:
Lately, there’s been a growing sense of confidence among investors that the U.S. economy has dodged the proverbial bullet. Despite a historic rate-hiking cycle by the Federal Reserve, two years of stubborn inflation, and signs of strain in global trade, the dominant Wall Street narrative is now a curious mix of “soft landing,” “no landing,” and even “re-acceleration.” For example, look at forward earnings estimates for the companies most susceptible to economic growth: small and mid-capitalization stocks. Over the last two years, earnings growth was meager when the economy was “booming” due to massive fiscal and monetary stimulus flows. However, as we head into 2026, Wall Street expects corporate earnings to increase sharply, which can only occur if the economy reaccelerates.

While the market is betting on an economic revival to support current valuation levels, the real economy is suggesting things are slowing down. Notably, the evidence isn’t coming from obscure corners. It’s showing up in the indicators designed to give us a heads-up before a storm arrives.

Unlike data points like GDP or headline payroll numbers that lag, indicators that lead economic changes are meant to flag when momentum is shifting. Historically, these indicators have done an excellent job of predicting economic changes from growth to slowdowns. However, after 2020, with the massive stimulus interventions, the leading indicators have been “missing in action.” Because of that, very few people are paying attention to what these indicators are warning about, and the current optimism feels eerily similar to the late stages of every economic cycle. When markets are up, investors ignore risk, and consider fundamentals irrelevant. But if you know where to look, the warnings are there, and you must be willing to step outside the bullish echo chamber.

There’s a familiar rhythm to economic slowdowns, and we’re hearing it again: first, the data softens quietly; then the revisions come in negative; credit starts tightening; consumer behavior grows cautious; and eventually, the labor market breaks. When everyone finally admits what’s happening, markets have already priced in the damage. That’s why it’s critical to understand what the signals are saying now and not after the fact.

So, let’s take a closer look at the economic dashboard. From my perspective, the economy is not accelerating but slowing down.

Economic Signals

The clearest sign of economic deceleration is the Conference Board’s Leading Economic Index (LEI), which has declined for 17 consecutive months. That’s not a rounding error; that’s the longest losing streak since the 2008 financial crisis. The LEI includes forward-looking components like new orders, jobless claims, building permits, and consumer expectations. Historically, when the index falls this far for this long, a recession isn’t a matter of “if,” but “when.” Even the Conference Board has publicly projected a contraction in late 2024 or early 2025, but that didn’t happen because of all the fiscal stimulus circulating through the economy. However, that stimulus has ended. The LEI has turned lower again, signaling a pickup in economic weakness. While the market largely ignores this flashing red light because the S&P keeps rising, it is a dangerous divergence that investors should consider.

The bond market is also warning us. The yield curve, specifically the spread between 2-year and 10-year Treasury yields, was inverted for over a year. However, it isn’t the inversion that indicates a recessionary onset; it is the UN-inversion. Every post-war recession has been preceded by an inversion, and a UN-inversion of the yield curve. Most people miss the fact that the inversion itself isn’t the recession; it’s the warning. The real trouble begins when the curve steepens again. Often, this is because the Fed is forced to cut rates in a panic. And guess what? As of this writing, markets are pricing at least 50 basis points of cuts by year-end. That doesn’t happen in a “re-accelerating” economy.

Then there’s the labor market. On the surface, it still looks healthy. But dig deeper, and the cracks are widening. The Bureau of Labor Statistics recently revised previous job gains by over 911,000. Temporary help services continue to decline, and job openings are down roughly 30% from their peak. However, the real number to watch is the percentage of full-time employment. Since full-time employment supports economic growth, it is unsurprising that there is a decent correlation between that measure and GDP. These are not random data points; they are classic early signs that job growth has peaked.

Credit markets are tightening across the board. The Fed’s Senior Loan Officer Survey shows that banks are making it harder to get consumer and commercial loans. Notably, credit is the oxygen of economic growth, and when it gets harder to borrow, businesses delay hiring and capital expenditures as consumers pull back. In addition, there are rising delinquency rates on credit cards and auto loans, particularly among lower-income households, as the post-COVID stimulus buffer runs out.

Consumer sentiment also continues to deteriorate. The University of Michigan’s latest survey shows a renewed drop in optimism, especially among lower-income groups who are being squeezed by higher prices, student loan repayments, and rising rents. Yes, spending has not collapsed, but that is mainly because consumers are running up credit balances while drawing down what’s left of their savings. This divergence between weak sentiment and sustained spending is not sustainable; eventually, the math breaks.

Given that consumption drives earnings, the slowdown in personal consumption expenditures (PCE) should serve as a warning to more bullish analysts’ forecasts.

Investing When There Are Slowdown Signals

Taken in isolation, each of these signals might be dismissed as noise. But taken together, they paint a consistent picture that the U.S. economy is slowing, and the risk of a traditional, cyclical recession is rising steadily. The trouble is, investors have been lulled into complacency by short-term resilience in the stock market and the memory of pandemic-era policy responses. However, those policy levers may not be as easy to pull now.

The combination of a weakening LEI, an inverted yield curve, tighter credit conditions, softening labor data, falling consumer confidence, and narrowing corporate profit margins doesn’t suggest a soft landing; it indicates the landing zone is shrinking quickly and the Federal Reserve is likely already behind the curve. Crucially, we must not forget that leading indicators, by their nature, flash warnings before the damage is visible. It’s often too late to adjust when these trends show up in GDP or official recession declarations.

One of investors’ biggest mistakes at this cycle stage is anchoring to outdated narratives. They point to low unemployment and solid consumer spending and argue that the worst is behind us. However, those are lagging data points. Unemployment always stays low, right up until it spikes. Spending always looks fine, right up until credit dries up. The market will have already moved if we wait for the headlines to confirm the slowdown.

For investors, the path forward is less about panic and more about preparation. Risk is best managed before it becomes obvious, and currently, that means reassessing portfolio positioning with a greater focus on quality, liquidity, and resilience.

  1. Shift toward higher-quality equities—companies with strong balance sheets, positive cash flow, and stable earnings. In late-cycle environments, businesses that can defend margins and avoid excessive leverage tend to outperform. Defensive sectors like healthcare, utilities, and consumer staples historically perform well in economic slowdowns. These aren’t the exciting momentum plays, but they often hold up better when growth stalls.
  2. Cash is no longer a drag. With money market funds and Treasury bills yielding north of 5%, holding cash offers income and flexibility. In a market that’s likely to become more volatile, the ability to act without selling at a loss becomes a competitive advantage. Don’t let the fear of “missing out” cause you to miss what may be the opportunity to buy later at better prices.
  3. Consider hedging. If you’ve enjoyed substantial gains in the past year, now is the time to protect some of those profits. This could mean trimming back riskier holdings, using inverse ETFs to hedge sector exposure, or selecting options to reduce downside risk. The goal isn’t to go all-in or all-out; it’s to manage exposure with a clear eye on growing macro uncertainty.
  4. Reduce exposure to highly cyclical sectors. Industries tied to discretionary spending, real estate, or manufacturing tend to underperform during economic contractions. If the slowdown intensifies, these areas will likely see earnings revisions and price multiple compression.
  5. Stay tactical. This isn’t the environment for set-it-and-forget-it strategies. Flexibility becomes critical in periods of transition. Markets are forward-looking, and asset prices will have already adjusted by the time the recession shows up in the data. Use this time to tighten your process, raise select cash, and rotate toward areas that can weather a macro storm.

The fiscal and monetary buffers that helped cushion previous slowdowns may not be available now. The federal government is already running massive deficits, and interest payments on the debt are now the fastest-growing budget item. On the monetary side, the Fed is caught between two mandates: inflation isn’t fully tamed, but growth is slowing. Cutting rates too soon risks reigniting price pressures; waiting too long deepens a downturn.

So while the S&P may continue to climb for now, and sentiment might stay upbeat, the forward-looking data suggests that caution may be a more prudent approach.

data.title: PennyMac Mortgage Investment Trust Offers Investors 3 Notes: 1 Gets A Buy Rating
data.description:
Introduction

Based on read counts on my recent mREIT preferred stocks and Notes articles, I’m returning to PennyMac Mortgage Investment Trust (NYSE:PMT). My last review covered the PennyMac Mortgage Investment Trust 8.50% Senior Notes due 9/30/2028 (NYSE:PMTU) where I compared it to other mREIT issuers Notes (see link below). This time I decided to compare all three PMT Notes, the other two being:

  • PennyMac Mortgage Investment Trust 9.00% Senior Notes due 2/15/2030 (NYSE:PMTV).
  • PennyMac Mortgage Investment Trust 9.00% Senior Notes due 6/15/2030 (NYSE:PMTW).

PMTW offers investors the best value in yield, YTC, YTM, and Call protection, so just considering these Notes and the near 9% yield, I gave PMTW the Buy rating.

By now, most Seeking Alpha fans have heard that the Federal Reserve cut its benchmark interest rate this month for the first time since December and signaled more cuts are likely this year. The FOMC opted for a 25bps cut this time around, though some voted for a 50bps reduction.

PennyMac Mortgage Investment Trust review

However you determine default risk, it is an important step in the due diligence process, but first, a brief overview of the issuer. Seeking Alpha describes this mREIT as (edited):

PennyMac Mortgage Investment Trust, through its subsidiary, primarily invests in residential mortgage-related assets in the United States. The company operates through: Credit Sensitive Strategies, Interest Rate Sensitive Strategies, Correspondent Production segments. The Credit Sensitive Strategies segment invests in credit risk transfer (CRT) agreements and subordinate mortgage-backed securities (MBS). The Interest Rate Sensitive Strategies segment engages in investing in mortgage servicing rights, base servicing and excess servicing spreads, and agency and senior non-agency MBS, as well as related interest rate hedging activities. The Correspondent Production segment is involved in purchasing, pooling, and reselling newly originated prime credit quality loans directly or in the form of MBS. The company primarily sells its loans to government-sponsored entities. The company started in 2009.

PennyMac provides this description about themselves:

As a real estate investment trust (REIT), our objective is to provide attractive risk-adjusted returns to our shareholders over the long-term, primarily through dividends and secondarily through capital appreciation. Our investment focus is on mortgage-related assets that we create through our industry-leading correspondent production activities, including mortgage servicing rights (MSRs). In correspondent production, we acquire, pool and securitize or sell newly originated prime credit quality loans.

Source: pmt.pennymac

While profitable, PMT seldom covers their common stock dividends. Of course, interest on the Notes is paid before the EPS calculations, but better earnings would provide comfort to Noteholders as I see it.

PMT at least has held steady in the value listed as Total Common Equity, which shows a 4.4X coverage ratio to the three Notes presented here: decent but not great.

Comparing the Notes

Factor PMTU PMTV PMTW
Issued 9/18/23 2/11/25 6/2/25
Size. (MM) $50 $150 $100
Coupon 8.50% 9.00% 9.00%
Call date 9/30/25 2/15/27 6/15/27
Maturity date 9/30/28 2/15/30 6/15/30
Price $25.29 $25.48 $25.33

Yield

 8.40% 8.83% 8.88%

YTC/YTM

 NEG/7.87% 7.53%/8.47% 8.17%/8.66%

The short life of each issue indicates to me that PMT thought they could either issue replacement Notes at lower coupons or that cash flows by each Note's maturity date wouldn’t require a new Note to be issued at all or for a smaller amount.

PMTW offers investors the best value in yield, YTC, YTM, and Call protection, so just considering these Notes and the near 9% yield, I gave PMTW the Buy rating.

PMTU will be Callable the week after this article publishes. With a coupon above all the PMT preferred stocks, it would be the first Called as I see it. If it isn't Called by early 2026, that might indicate PMT does not have the ability to refinance it at a lower coupon. Something investors should keep an eye on.

Portfolio strategy

Investors have three options on how to invest in PennyMac, each with very different return and risk profiles, these being:

  • Common stock: Of the three, the common shares ranked the lowest and thus come with the highest default risk. They also carry the highest chance of investors having the dividend cut or eliminated. For those reasons, the common shareholders currently collect a 13.2% yield.
  • Preferred stock: Ranking above the common stock are the various preferred issues, thus the name. If a dividend is skipped, common stock dividends must stop, and any skipped cumulative preferred dividend must be paid before common stock dividends can be reinstated. All the PMT issues come with Call risk.
  • Notes: This asset class has the least risk by several measures. First, they rank above the other two in case of bankruptcy. Unlike dividends, their interest payments cannot be skipped as easily, probably requiring bankruptcy to do so. While they, like most preferred stocks, come with Call risk, since they have a maturity date, and assuming they're held until then, an investor knows their YTM on every purchase.

Final thoughts

PMT preferred stock stockholders experienced a hidden risk. When the LIBOR ending, PMT decided not to convert to SOFR, instead making the fixed rate permanent, eliminating the floating rate formula. They weren’t the only issuer to take that path, but they were the one that seems to have generated the most investor ire (article link). As other firms did the same, the market must have accepted that action, as PMT has issued both PMTV and PMTW since then, though at 50bps higher coupon than PMTU issued in 2023.

As I mentioned above, just over a year ago I compared the PMT Note against other mREIT issuers’ Notes and gave it a Hold rating, as others provided better yields across all three measures (current, YTC, and YTM). Glancing at current yields, that still seems to be the case, but default risk probably varies across the different issues and needs to be evaluated to determine if the extra yield is enough: PMTU: Due To Better Note Choices, Rating Drops To Hold.

A recent article looked at risk/return from a different perspective: PMTU Vs. IBHH: Evaluating Single Note To HY Bond ETF.

data.title: Virtus InfraCap U.S. Preferred Stock ETF Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

In the second quarter of 2025, the S&P U.S. Preferred Stock Index (Benchmark Index) returned 2.31%, underperforming the S&P 500® Index’s 10.94%, which was led higher by common stock recoveries post-“Liberation Day.” We view the original proposed tariffs as unlikely, and expect a softening stance to continue over the next few months. We continue to be bullish on bonds, with a 3.75% year-end yield target on the 10-year.

How the fund performed

For the quarter, the Fund (NYSEARCA:PFFA) returned -0.10% on net asset value (NAV), while the Benchmark Index returned 2.31%. The effect of selection contributed -2.13% to the return, allocation detracted -0.32%, and leverage and related interest expenses added 0.24%.

The Fund paid a monthly dividend of $0.17 per share for each month of the quarter. NAV at quarter-end was $20.57. The Fund had a 30-day SEC yield1 of 9.73%. This annualized figure reflects the distributions and dividends received during the period, after the deduction of Fund expenses.

The Fund’s relative performance was mostly impacted by its underweight in mandatory convertible preferred stocks, which trade more similarly to common equity than fixed income. For example, the Boeing (BA) mandatory preferred, the largest weighted position in the Benchmark Index at 4.0%, returned 16.8% during the quarter. This was the primary driver of our relative underperformance.

At the end of the quarter, our non-bank financial services allocation was 39.3%, compared to 25.3% for the Benchmark Index. Our view is that the yield and credit profiles of this sector are attractive to hold through the market cycle. The Fund maintained its overweight to real estate with a weighting of 19.8% compared to 5.6% for the Benchmark Index. We believe that real estate and certain financial services sectors have better risk-adjusted total return opportunities. The Fund ended the quarter with a leverage of 22.5% of NAV, below the trailing 12-month average of 23.2%. At quarter-end, the Fund had a 23.4% and 13.4% weighting in fixed-to-floating-rate and floating-rate preferreds, respectively.

The Fund expects to pay a monthly dividend of $0.17 per share with a focus on maximizing yield-to-call and optimization profits. The use of leverage also contributes to the amount of income available for distribution.

Top Contributors

Diversified Healthcare Trust (DHC) operates as a health care real estate investment trust (REIT). The REIT owns office buildings leased to medical providers, medical-related businesses, clinics and biotech laboratory tenants, senior living communities, and wellness centers. Diversified Healthcare Trust serves customers in the United States.

Babcock & Wilcox Enterprises, Inc. (BW) provides power generation equipment. The company offers water-tube boilers, environmental systems, steam, and other related products. Babcock & Wilcox Enterprises serves industries worldwide.

Lumen Technologies, Inc. (LUMN) provides digital solutions for home and business premises. The company offers communications, network security, cloud solutions, voice, and managed services. Lumen Technologies serves customers worldwide.

KKR & Co. Inc. (KKR) operates as an investment company. The company invests in private equity, credit, capital markets, infrastructure, insurance, energy, health care, utilities, and real estate companies. KKR serves clients worldwide.

Bottom Contributors

Flagstar Financial, Inc. (FLG) is a multi-bank holding company. The company, through its banking subsidiaries, offers a full range of traditional and non-traditional banking, mortgage, business, and private banking solutions. Flagstar Financial serves customers in the United States.

Compass Diversified Holdings (CODI) is an investment holding company. The company acquires controlling interests in profitable small- to middle-market businesses in niche industries, and works with management to pursue growth opportunities and provide strategic support.

The Boeing Company operates as an aerospace company. The company develops, manufactures, and services commercial airplanes, defense products, and space systems. Boeing serves clients worldwide.

DigitalBridge Group, Inc. (DBRG) operates as a digital infrastructure firm. The company invests in data centers, cell towers, fiber networks, small cells, and edge infrastructure. DigitalBridge Group serves customers in the United States.

Portfolio Discussion

During the quarter, we maintained our overweight positions in real estate and underweight positions in banks. New issuance began to ramp up during the quarter, following a strong recovery in equity markets. The Fund continued to take advantage of index rebalancing strategies, as well as short-term pricing inefficiencies. In anticipation of expected market volatility, the Fund has continued to opportunistically draw down leverage.

Outlook

We continue to actively position the portfolio to potentially benefit from current market and economic environments. We do not expect a U.S. recession, and we reiterate our stagdeflation call. The critical determinants of inflation, money supply growth, and energy prices are declining year-over-year. In fact, the nearly 15% decline in energy prices more than offsets any potential increases from tariffs.

Importantly, declines in energy prices bleed through to core holdings, as all businesses use substantial energy. We continue to project that the U.S. Federal Reserve will cut two to three times this year. Inflation data continues to print cool, and the labor market is decelerating, as ultra-tight monetary policy takes a toll on the housing and construction markets. We continue to be bullish on Treasury bonds, despite the recent sell-off with a 3.75% year-end yield target on the 10-year. The best cure for higher rates is higher rates, as rates over 4.5% on the 10-year imply a 30-year mortgage rate over 7%, which will cause the housing market to further slow.

The Total Expense Ratio represents the Fund’s Total Annual Fund Operating Expenses, which includes the management fee and other expenses where applicable, except for certain payments that are paid directly by the Fund, as described in the Prospectus.

The Fund is an exchange-traded fund (“ETF”). The “net asset value” (NAV) of the Fund is determined at the close of each business day, and represents the dollar value of one share of the Fund; it is calculated by taking the total assets of the Fund, subtracting total liabilities, and dividing by the total number of shares outstanding. The NAV of the Fund is not necessarily the same as its intraday trading value. Fund investors should not expect to buy or sell shares at NAV because shares of ETFs such as the Fund are bought and sold at market price (not NAV) and are not individually redeemed from the Fund. Thus, shares may trade at a premium or discount to their NAV in the secondary market. Brokerage commissions will reduce returns. NAV returns are calculated using the Fund’s daily 4:00 pm NAV, and include the reinvestment of all dividends and other distributions (reinvested at the Fund’s NAV on distribution ex-date). Market price returns are calculated using the 4:00 pm midpoint between the bid and offer, and include the reinvestment of all dividends and other distributions (reinvested at the 4:00 pm bid/offer midpoint on distribution ex-date). Market price returns do not represent the return you would receive if you traded at other times.

Benchmark: The S&P U.S. Preferred Stock Index measures performance of the U.S. preferred stock market. Preferred stocks pay dividends at a specified rate and receive preference over common stocks in terms of dividend payments and liquidation of assets. The index is calculated on a total return basis with dividend reinvested. The index is unmanaged, its returns do not reflect any fees, expenses, or sales charges, and is not available for direct investment.

The Fund is an actively managed exchange-traded fund and does not seek to replicate the performance of a specified index. The Fund may have a higher portfolio turnover than funds that seek to replicate the performance of an index.

IOPV, or Indicative Optimized Portfolio Value, is a calculation disseminated by the stock exchange that approximates the Fund’s NAV every fifteen seconds throughout the trading day.

Notes on Risk: Exchange-Traded Funds (ETF): The value of an ETF may be more volatile than the underlying portfolio of securities it is designed to track. The costs to the portfolio of owning shares of an ETF may exceed the cost of investing directly in the underlying securities. Preferred Stocks: Preferred stocks may decline in price, fail to pay dividends, or be

illiquid. Leverage: When the Fund leverages its portfolio, the Fund may be less liquid and/or may liquidate positions at an unfavorable time, and the value of the Fund’s shares will be more volatile and sensitive to market movements. Non-Diversified: The portfolio is not diversified and may be more susceptible to factors negatively impacting its holdings to the extent the portfolio invests more of its assets in the securities of fewer issuers than would a diversified portfolio. Market Price/NAV: At the time of purchase and/or sale, an investor’s shares may have a market price that is above or below the fund’s NAV, which may increase the investor’s risk of loss.
