data.title: The Simply Good Foods: Margins In Retreat And A Valuation Under Review
data.description:
Monetary policy, inflation, and the impact on Simply Good Foods

After Jerome Powell’s latest announcement, it seems that monetary policy in the United States is showing signs of a shift after the recent cycle of aggressive hikes. In fact, the latest dot plot projects a level of 3.50%-3.75% by the end of this year, and with additional steps in 2026 to reach around 3.25%-3.50%. Good news for companies with some leverage, which will notice the relief in the cost of debt.

I also want to highlight that the Food at Home index registered a 2.7% YoY increase this August 2025. These are more contained levels compared to the inflation seen in 2022, but it is worth closely monitoring a curve that is starting to show signs of turning upward again.

In theory, this context makes companies like The Simply Good Foods Company (NASDAQ:SMPL) depend more on volume and innovation in new product formats rather than benefiting from a cost inflation environment.

For example, the healthy snacks category, which is part of the company’s product portfolio, still shows traction despite the overall cooling in consumption. In fact, the U.S. market is expected to grow around 4.8% YoY, reaching approximately $156B this year. Adding to this the fact that consumers are choosing healthier options than in previous years, it can be said that the volume part may be covered. At least for now.

Logically, SMPL has to compete in a market where private labels are, in many cases, perceived by consumers as quality products, and that limits pricing power, forcing the company to optimize processes to improve margins. For instance, reference companies such as BellRing Brands (BRBR) trade at a FWD EV/EBITDA of 11x, compared to SMPL's multiple of 10x. Data that seem to confirm the hypotheses I am raising.

How has its market performance been then? I have observed that in the last 12 months, the Dow Jones US Food Products index is down -18%, but SMPL has done even worse with -23.2%. I believe that if the sector benchmark reflects this adjustment of lower growth due to multiple compression and contained margins, this additional underperformance of SMPL may be due to guidance that raises doubts about its implementation, or perhaps to the perception that the company lacks a differentiated value proposition to increase market share via product.

Quest, Atkins, and OWYN: the revenue engine

It is now time to put into context what SMPL sells, since its business is mainly concentrated in protein bars, shakes, and snacks that are distributed in large retailers or digital channels. Basically, it is a model centered on branded products that are mass-distributed.

Its revenue mix is concentrated in Quest and Atkins, which represent 95% of FY24 revenues. Quest is the brand that, in my view, offers the most innovation through ready-to-drink beverages, low-sugar snacks, chips, and protein bars. Then comes Atkins, a more consolidated brand that provides greater stability to revenues. And finally, OWYN, with plant-based products, which was incorporated in 2024 and which, according to guidance, is expected to contribute 10% to the revenue mix in FY25.

And this is where I see the moat the company tries to defend coming into play, because against the pressure from private labels, SMPL leans on the intangibles of its brands, with leading products such as protein bars, which are intended to sustain margins in volatile cost environments.

As for the shareholder structure, I would not highlight anything relevant. There are no shareholders that control the company through dual-class shares or similar institutional control, so the free float is high.

Finally, at the operational level, SMPL relies almost entirely on co-manufacturers for the production of its products, which I see as an advantage, as it allows the company to launch new products quickly in such a dynamic sector. In addition, it keeps CapEx below 1% of sales.

The problem I see with this asset-light model is that it exposes the company to potential price increases or a lack of production capacity for any reason, and forces it to constantly monitor its suppliers and evaluate alternatives in the event of unforeseen events.

Margins in decline and the integration of OWYN

To begin valuing SMPL, I have to explain, from my way of understanding accounting, which adjustments need to be made to see the real picture of the business.

After the acquisition of OWYN, the company revalued its inventories to market value, and amortizes them through COGS as that stock is sold, which increases cost of sales by $1.4M when that inventory rotates. In addition, there are integration costs that amount to approximately $12.1M, as well as accumulated stock-based compensation of $12.8M.

Obviously, none of these items reflect SMPL’s core operations, and yet they all impact EBITDA, which is why I have to use a metric that allows me to value, in a faithful and realistic way, the company’s cash generation.

Reviewing the last quarter’s numbers, I have seen that the sales engine comes from Quest with $227.7M, followed by Atkins with $112.3M and OWYN contributing $33.6M, closing the quarter with +13.8% YoY. In my opinion, this is good product traction, and not so much the effect of a model based on price increases.

However, I also see weaker margins, with the quarterly gross profit margin falling to 36.4% from the previous 39.9%. The reasons have been several, such as the higher cost of cocoa, new tariffs, and the lower margin of OWYN, which has reduced the group’s average.

As for cash, it has been weaker than in 2024 due to inventory build-up, according to the company’s own report. I do not yet see problems in its asset-light model, and it continues to show good conversion, so I see this more as temporary pressure from the integration of OWYN. But we must pay attention to whether the improvements management expects will materialize in the first half of FY26.

And regarding the balance sheet, I see debt contained at around $152M net, so I place the focus on inventories. It holds around $165M in stock, and here the sensitivity is considerable, since, for example, a 5% increase in inventory means about $8M of immobilized assets. This is money that is no longer available as cash, and, therefore, ends up reducing FCF. In my view, this ability to control and rotate inventories is where the main focus should be.

SMPL Valuation: EV/EBITDA multiples and 2025–2026 price target

Based on the results I have just shown, and as of the date of writing this article, I start with a market cap of $2.64B, 100.8M shares, and an EV of $2.79B.

The metric I will use to value SMPL is adjusted EV/EBITDA in order to avoid distortions from amortizations or integration costs. According to the company’s guidance, a 200 bp margin cut and a weaker Q4 are anticipated, so to be conservative, we could place forward EBITDA at $255M or $265M.

If I compare it with BellRing, which trades at a multiple of 11x, or Hershey (HSY) at 18x, SMPL's historical range of between 10x and 15x is a reasonable range where it should be situated, though currently more on the lower side. Therefore, when translating this into a reasonable per-share price, it would mean applying an 11x multiple to $265M of forward EBITDA, which leaves the price at about $27 or $29.

Whether the market assigns a multiple closer to 10x or 13x will depend on factors such as recovering gross margin and increasing traction from OWYN in the revenue mix. To broaden the range a bit, it would be a price of about $25 or $26 on the lower end, and $30 or $32 on the higher end, depending on execution in the coming quarters.

Now, for the market to reward with multiples of 12x-12.5x, it will be essential that both catalysts materialize; if they do not, the stock will remain anchored to the lower range of 10.5x-11x EV/EBITDA. My overall reading indicates that the market is already pricing in caution, and with this valuation on the table, the next step is to analyze which risks or catalysts could alter this scenario.

From my point of view, and especially considering that management expects weaker growth, I lean more toward multiples closer to 10x, that is, the $25 range, so there would not be much upside at current prices.

Key risks and growth catalysts

In my opinion, inventory turnover and debt management are the main risks for the company to continue bleeding at the stock market level. As we mentioned earlier, every 5% increase in inventories is practically $8M in cash tied up.

In addition, although net leverage is at a level that I consider comfortable for SMPL, around 0.5x EBITDA, the term loan is referenced to a variable rate. This means that every 100 bp increase in interest rates will raise its financial costs by about $2.5M.

These would be small adjustments in the balance sheet and debt, but in a business where I see margins under increasing pressure, it can cause the multiple to compress further, and it would not be unreasonable to justify a multiple of 8x or 9x EV/EBITDA.

On the other hand, in a bullish scenario, if SMPL frees up cash by reducing stock, it could be used for debt repayment or share repurchase programs. Another point would be the synergies with OWYN, which could return 100 bp to the gross margin in the coming quarters and once again justify the upper part of the range.

This, added to the traction of Quest in RTD beverages, and with OWYN growing +20% as in recent quarters, my opinion would be to reward this execution and place a multiple around 13x EV/EBITDA, with a price of $32 per share.

SMPL: between market patience and margin recovery

I lean toward a Hold rating for The Simply Good Foods Company.

I believe the company can defend a range between $25 and $27. Quest continues to show good traction, and OWYN continues to grow at double digits, representing almost 10% of the revenue mix. The problem comes from margins and from working capital, which requires good discipline to avoid locking up too much cash in inventories.

Debt remains in a range of 0.5x EBITDA, so I do not see excessive concern on this side.

So my final opinion is that for the market to return to multiples of 12x or 13x EV/EBITDA, in the coming quarters, there must be a recovery or stabilization in margins, confirmation that the company can release stock with ease, and continued traction from Quest and OWYN. If so, levels above $31 could be recovered with relative ease.

But for now, I prefer to be cautious and stay at Hold, until we see how management executes on these factors.

data.title: OppFi: Deep Value In Non‑Prime Lending, With Possible Regulatory Storm Clouds
data.description:
Introduction

OppFi Inc. (NYSE:OPFI) is a technology‑driven specialty finance company focused on providing unsecured installment loans to borrowers who can't access mainstream credit. Through its partnerships with banks, OppFi has built a digital lending platform that takes on the high‑cost loan niche dominated by names like Enova International, Oportun and LendingClub. Over the past year the stock has traded between $4.34 and $17.73 and recently settled near $11.74, with a market cap of roughly $1.0 billion.

In this article I will walk through OppFi's business model, the latest financial results and the key risks. With improving profitability and a valuation that looks inexpensive relative to peers, I think the shares offer an attractive buying opportunity. Still, regulatory overhang, a complicated Up-C structure, persistent insider selling, a competitive FinTech environment, and exposure to economic downturns makes me reluctant to take a position.

Business model and products

OppFi's core product is OppLoans: a small‑dollar installment loan arranged through FinWise Bank. Typical loans run between $500 and $4,000 over 9‑ to 18‑month terms with annual percentage rates of between 160 and 179%. These are expensive loans, but they provide crucial credit access for borrowers who have nowhere else to turn.

Since OppFi is not a bank, they partner with FinWise and other banks to originate loans, then purchases 95% of the receivables. These loans are then bundled into securities and sold to institutional investors. Loans are underwritten through machine learning underwriting ("Model 6") and high automation allow fast credit decisions. Despite incredibly high APRs and charge offs, the company reports a 4.5/5 customer rating on Trustpilot and says that auto‑approval rates climbed from 72% in 2023 to 79% in 2024 and 80% by Q2 2025.

On top of this flagship business, OppFi has launched and experimented with a handful of complementary products. SalaryTap was introduced in 2021 and offers larger loans ($2,000 to $5,000) at prime‑like rates with repayments via payroll deduction. It's still small but showcases how the company can move into lower‑APR products over time. OppFi also holds a 35% stake in Bitty Holdings investment, a small business credit provider.

Operating metrics and financial performance

OppFi's financial results reflect steady growth and improving credit performance. In 2024 the company recorded $526mn in total revenue, an increase of 7.4% over 2023, while net income reached $83.8mn dollars, more than double the prior year. In the fourth quarter of 2024, revenue was $135.7mn, up about 2%, and net income rose to $13.9mn. Management attributed the quarterly improvement to lower net charge‑offs and higher yields.

The first quarter of 2025 continued this momentum. Total revenue grew to $140.3mn, up 10% year over year. Net income reached $20.4mn and adjusted net income (excluding stock‑based compensation and certain one‑time costs) was $33.8mn. The net charge‑off rate declined to 34.6%, more than thirteen percentage points lower than the prior year. Total expenses were about 34% of revenue, and loan originations totaled $189mn, leaving OppFi with $407mn in receivables at quarter‑end.

On 6 August 2025 the company reported its second‑quarter results and raised full‑year guidance. Quarterly revenue increased 12.8% to $142.4mn. GAAP net income was $11.5mn, lower than the prior year primarily because of non-cash revaluation of warrant liabilities, while adjusted net income climbed 59% to $39.4mn. Adjusted earnings per share were $0.45 versus $0.29 a year earlier. The net charge‑off rate improved to 31.9%, and the average yield reached 136.1%. OppFi ended the quarter with $437.8mn in receivables and $423mn in net originations, and the auto‑approval rate rose to 80%. Following these results, management increased its 2025 revenue guidance to $578-605mn and its adjusted net income guidance to $125-130mn, with adjusted EPS now expected at $1.39-1.44.

OppFi's operating model rests on several factors. The company uses proprietary machine‑learning underwriting, and its "Model 6" algorithm has allowed more automated approvals without loosening credit standards. Customer‑satisfaction scores are high as management reported a Net Promoter Score of 79 in Q1 2025, and automation helps keep operating expenses relatively low. OppFi is also diversifying beyond high‑APR loans: it offers a payroll‑deducted SalaryTap product and holds a 35% stake in Bitty, a small‑business credit platform.

OppFi funds its receivables mainly through securitizations and secured credit facilities. As of Q2'25 the company reported restricted and unrestricted cash at $78.3mn and $219.1mn of unused debt capacity under its financing facilities, which translates into roughly $603.3 million of total funding capacity.

Valuation

At around $11.74 per share, OppFi's market capitalization is roughly $1.025bn. Management expects 2025 adjusted net income of $125-130mn and adjusted EPS of $1.39-1.44. On a EV/Revenue basis, shares look reasonably priced.

On an absolute basis, it is very hard to argue that OppFi is anything other than cheap. The reverse DCF below forces us to employ some very conservative assumptions (13% discount rate, 2% growth, and 22% FCF margin) to get to the present share price.

This assumes analyst estimates until 2026 and the assumptions in the table thereafter. In my opinion, OppFi looks cheap on an absolute basis, as it would not be outlandish to assume a short term growth rate of around 25-40% and FCF margins in the same ballpark.

Risks and Why I Think Its a Hold

OppFi's business depends on a partnership with Utah-based FinWise Bank, which lets it offer loans nationwide at higher rates than many states normally allow. Regulators have been challenging this setup, and pressure has grown since 2024.

California's Department of Financial Protection and Innovation (DFPI) filed a cross‑complaint in 2022 alleging that OppFi-not FinWise-is the "true lender," seeking to void loans above the state's 36% rate cap and impose at least $100mn in penalties. Several federal class actions alleging OppFi is the true lender have been stayed or sent to arbitration after the Ninth Circuit upheld OppFi's arbitration clause, but state regulators are not bound by these outcomes.

There are also possible legislative headwinds. Members of Congress periodically propose a national 36% APR cap (such as the Veterans and Consumers Fair Credit Act). While such bills have not yet entered the books, bipartisan concern over high‑cost lending and data privacy may lead to new legislative efforts.

In summary, rate caps will limit OppFi's growth potential and could hurt its current market share if future litigation against the company is successful. This risk is quite unique to OppFi's "Rent-a-Bank" model, but could also affect companies like Dave (DAVE) whose APRs far exceed regulatory limits if all the fees paid were to qualify as interest payments.

Note that, should federal and state legislatures turn against this model, it would undermine OppFi's entire business. This risk is also embedded in a host of other factors that may justify the current share price, such as significant exposure to economic downturns, a competitive FinTech environment, persistent insider selling, and a complex Up-C structure that can turn off investors.

Conclusion

OppFi has evolved from a niche online lender into a profitable, technology‑enabled platform. In both Q1 and Q2 2025 the company delivered record revenue and adjusted earnings and raised full‑year guidance. Higher auto‑approval rates, better credit models and lower charge‑offs have turbocharged margins, while robust liquidity and a growing share‑repurchase program show management's conviction.

That said, regulatory risk is the elephant in the room. True‑lender lawsuits and potential state or federal rate caps could disrupt the bank‑partnership model. OppFi also serves non‑prime borrowers, so a weakening macro environment could pressure credit performance. I am not too comfortable or familiar with the Up-C structure. Still, with a forward P/E around 8× and a free‑cash‑flow yield near 19%, there seems to be a cushion of safety. If litigation risk abates and earnings compound, OppFi's multiple could expand toward peer levels. A small position could be warranted, but I prefer to stay away.

data.title: I Could Build My Entire Retirement Around These 4 Stocks
data.description:
Introduction

The other day, someone told me something like, "Why do you spend so much time discussing retirement investments? You’re never going to retire, anyway!"

It's a fair question, for a number of reasons. The first one is that, as I have written in a few articles, I am indeed not ever planning on retiring.

Although I have no idea what the future holds when it comes to AI disruption, my health, and other circumstances, I would love to do what Buffett and so many others do, which is work until it’s no longer humanly possible. After all, my work is based on writing research on a computer. I’m not a construction worker or a Texas roughneck. That’s a huge difference.

Even Maurice Greenberg, the 100-year-old who landed on D-Day in France during the Second World War, fought in the Korean War, and became the successor of AIG founder Cornelius Vander Starr, still works. Roughly ten years ago, he made a big comeback with a multi-billion-dollar insurance takeover. He was 90 at the time.

Obviously, I’m not comparing myself to people like Buffett and Greenberg. All I’m saying is that I’m a workaholic. I haven’t had a trip (some call it "vacation") in more than a decade where I did not spend every day working on a project, article, or similar endeavour.

The other reason I am not a huge fan of talking about my own retirement is motivation. Striving for the goal of financial independence is a great thing, don’t get me wrong. It eases some anxiety, as I will soon be able to stop working if I decide to, technically speaking.

However, on top of the fact that I would do exactly what I am doing right now if I suddenly got really wealthy, I also believe that not having a safety net is better for progress. Personally, I have never been more productive than in the years after I completely wrecked my trading portfolio while being in college. Back then, I traded leveraged bonds. It went really well until it didn’t, and the countermove destroyed my stop losses, leaving me with a hole that took me at least a year to fill.

The good news is that it caused me to rethink my entire strategy and work hard to become a better investor. It led to everything I am doing now.

Ok. That’s enough about me.

The reason I promised readers to focus more on retirement stocks is based on my belief that owning higher-yielding value stocks, in general, is a good idea, and because retirement planning is critical, given increasing pressure on Social Security, as I wrote this month.

Essentially, Social Security cash flows have become a disaster since the Great Financial Crisis, with increasing deficits and funding requirements, which could lead to cuts down the road and changes like delayed retirements and other regulatory changes.

As the CATO Institute wrote in its warning report, it may be important for people to take more control of their own retirement. As I am someone who is expected to get close to zero from my country’s Social Security and other pension plans, I could not agree more with that.

What if, instead of replacing earnings in old age regardless of need, Social Security was a basic safety net? And what if, instead of relying so heavily on a government program for income in retirement, Americans could own and control more of their own retirement savings, with Social Security acting as a poverty backstop? This reimagined Social Security system would be far from ideal but substantially better than the current unsustainable system. - CATO Institute

In this article, I’ll focus on that and present a few investments that I trust so much, I could make them the core of my retirement portfolio.

So, let’s dive in!

How To Take Control Of Your Own Retirement?

I’m not a financial advisor. And even if I were, I could not give you personal advice, as this article is read by a huge range of different investors. Some are multi-millionaires with second and third homes. Others are barely making ends meet and investing as much as they can to somewhat improve their cash flows. Meanwhile, others have lived paycheck to paycheck their entire lives, while others have enjoyed big tailwinds like inheritances.

For example, if you inherit a home in Contra Costa County, California, you would likely look at a windfall of close to $800,000, based on the average home price in that region, as we can see below.

That’s why even during retirement, some can increase their wealth, others manage to keep it steady by generating enough cash flow, while others have to sell assets to cover expenses.

Anyway, my point is that everyone has different needs, which makes this topic so complex.

That’s why Fidelity made a roadmap. It all starts by asking how much money you need.

  • What lifestyle do you want to maintain?
  • What will your average monthly expenses be?
  • What about the risks of unexpected costs?
  • Have you thought about health expenditures?
  • What about retirement home costs (if needed)?
  • Do you have a pension?
  • What about 401(k)s?
  • What percentage of expenses will be covered by Social Security?

Fidelity says we should try to follow the 10x, which suggests we need to save 10x the salary we expect to make before we start retirement. If you make $80,000 a year, that number is $800,000. If you make $120,000, that number is (you guessed it), $1.2 million.

Now, the tricky part is that we need to incorporate wage growth and inflation. If you’re a 20-year-old college student reading this, you’ll have to factor in close to 50 years of inflation and wage growth. Just imagine what the average income will be in the year 2075 and how high your annual expenses could be. A million dollars by then won’t get you far.

Heck, depending on where you live right now, a million isn’t much either. While a million dollars makes you a king in countries like Albania, a 5-7% yield on that amount makes you lower-middle-class in areas like the San Francisco Bay Area (let’s stick with that example).

Here are some costs to keep in mind (based on Fidelity findings):

  • Health expenses (excluding long-term care) for retirees could be north of $170,000. That’s the total. Not per year (obviously). Personally, these costs freak me out the most, as I am currently witnessing just how stressed the healthcare system is getting in nations like the Netherlands, Germany, and other developed places. I can only imagine the premium hikes my insurance will execute if this continues.

As we can see below, healthcare is one of the few costs that will rise as we get older. Meanwhile, other costs like travel, entertainment, transportation, and food decline.

  • Another factor is inflation. Between 2009 and 2021 (more or less), inflation averaged less than 2.0%. In addition to paving the road for huge stock market gains, it also made retiring much easier. As I believe in "higher for longer" interest rates and inflation, I think we need to work at least with a base of 3-4% annual inflation.

To show you how big a deal small inflation changes are, let’s use the inflation calculator.

The purchasing power of $100,000 in 2025 equals $122,000 after 10 years based on 2% inflation. A 3% inflation rate brings that number to $134,000. That’s a 10% difference.

  • Longevity is another fascinating topic. Some people may have to fund three decades of retirement. Just imagine what can happen during these years. Think about all the technologies that were invented over the past ten years and how these disrupted markets. The Internet, AI, smartphones, etc. This means we need to buy the right assets that have competitive advantages and inflation protection. Also, if people can afford it, I always suggest that people keep investing for growth even after reaching retirement age. Bear in mind that Buffett generated the overwhelming majority of his wealth after reaching the legal retirement age, as the non-updated chart below shows.

Again, Buffett is a special case, but I think you get my point. Even a 2% dividend yield turns into a yield on cost of nearly 7% when compounded at 10% per year for two decades. If the stock price keeps up with these growth rates, it’s also a blessing for one’s total net worth.

  • What’s your risk profile? I have met people who have lost sleep over a 25% equity fund. I also know people who have the overwhelming majority of their multi-million net worth in crypto, tech stocks, and real estate, who don’t even break a sweat when things go down 20-40%. On top of prudent financial planning (what do I need in the future?), it’s a mindset thing.

See, the higher the risk, the higher the return. An "aggressive growth" portfolio of 85% equities returns close to 10% per year, historically speaking. However, during really bad recessions, it can temporarily lose more than half of its value (Great Financial Crisis).

A portfolio of 20% equities returns less than 6.0% per year. However, the odds of a steep decline are super low, as we can see below. Then there’s anything in between.

Interestingly enough, though, on a long-term basis, the odds of losing money with an aggressive stock portfolio are zero. Since the inception of the stock market in the U.S., nobody has ever experienced negative returns on a 20-year basis.

Could it change? Yes. Is it likely? No.

This is one of the reasons why I’m an "equity guy." Instead of bonds, I would rather buy bond-like stocks like utilities, consumer staples, and REITs, which brings me to the next part of this article.

The 4 Stocks I Would Trust As A Core

Last month, I wrote an article titled "The Only Retirement Investing Strategy I Would Trust With My Future." It was about building core holdings that bring income and stability. Once that has been taken care of, investors can use smaller holdings to increase their return or income profile. Right now, these could be investments in AI, the energy transition, economic re-shoring, or other ideas.

For most people, the core should consist of ETFs, as these provide the best diversification benefits. By buying 2-3 ETFs, investors can buy high-yield dividend income, bonds, and even higher-growth dividend stocks like the Vanguard Dividend Appreciation ETF (VIG).

As I do not invest, I would buy ETF-like companies with diversification benefits, wide-moat business models (or other benefits), and the ability to provide consistently rising income.

In my case, I would aim for an average yield of 5.0% to 5.5%, which is a rate that has various benefits:

  • If I were to achieve this return, I could retire without having to buy high-risk income stocks.
  • By aiming for a mid-single-digit dividend yield, we can also incorporate lower-yielding dividend growth stocks.
  • Essentially, falling for a "sucker yield" is much harder in this area. If I were forced to buy double-digit income to fund my retirement, the story would be different.

Right now, my core would definitely include ONEOK (OKE), a company I have brought up in a number of articles this year. ONEOK is a C-Corp midstream company that does not issue a K-1 form. It owns a massive network of pipelines and related support operations in areas like the Permian Basin, where it generates substantial income in fast-growing markets like natural gas liquids, natural gas gathering/processing, and refined products (see below).

Like most companies, its revenues are mostly coming from fixed-fee contracts. That’s one of the biggest benefits, as these companies are not directly dependent on the price of oil, gas, and related commodities. As a result, ONEOK has grown its EBITDA for more than 10 consecutive years with a >16% CAGR since 2013. This includes the 2014/2015 oil crash, the 2020 pandemic, as well as the current oil and gas price weakness.

Through at least 2026, the company aims for mid-to-high single-digit annual EPS growth.

Currently, OKE yields 5.8%.

Its dividend has not been cut for at least 25 years and is expected to grow by 3-4% per year.

Interestingly enough, the yield is only so high because of recent stock price weakness. The company trades 40% below its 52-week high, as a result of weak oil prices. While this does not directly impact its revenues, the market has started to fear that prolonged subdued prices could hurt output. So far, this has happened every time during downturns and provided great opportunities.

Then there’s Getty Realty (GTY), a company I have never brought up. It has a yield of 6.9%, a payout ratio of slightly less than 80%, and is one of the biggest owners of convenience and automotive retail assets. This includes auto service centers, drive-through restaurants, and car washes in high-demand areas.

Although some of these areas have elevated competition (think of car washes), the company has an excellent track record of occupancy (99.7% as of 2Q25), rent collection, and tenant rent coverage. This allowed it to grow its dividend by 5.1% per year since 2019.

It also has an investment-grade credit rating, a net leverage ratio of 5.2x, and an average weighted lease duration of a full decade.

Another REIT I would add is VICI Properties (VICI), the owner of major Las Vegas Strip properties like MGM Grand, Caesars Palace, Park MGM, Mandalay Bay, New York-New York, and others. It has a triple net lease business model and 100% rent collection.

Moreover, roughly half of its revenue is generated outside of Las Vegas, as the company is consistently diversifying into other entertainment areas with strong tenants, pricing power, and secular growth. See, while AI is disrupting the world, hard assets and entertainment are two areas I expect to thrive, as people want to enjoy life and spend on experiences.

With that said, while Las Vegas is under fire from plunging visitor numbers that have triggered layoffs, I believe there is no risk to VICI, as the city suffers from a mix of weak consumer sentiment and the fact that most resorts have used premium pricing to boost margins.

The biggest risk is for the resort operators to bring back customers. All will rely on their assets. This shields VICI and its investors. It also makes for a better yield, as VICI now yields 5.7% with mid-single-digit annual dividend growth.

Last but not least, I would add PepsiCo (PEP), a company I owned from 2020 until I sold it to free up cash for my Albania investment a few months ago (see this article). Currently yielding 4.0%, the company has been targeted by Elliott Management to improve its business.

The company, which owns some of the world’s most favorite snacks and drinks (think of Lay’s and Pepsi), is the biggest snack player in the world, with at least 20 billion-dollar brands that compete with companies like Coca-Cola (KO).

Because of somewhat underwhelming growth and pressure on margins in recent years, the poor stock price performance has created the biggest discount in at least two decades, as we can see below. Trading at just 18x earnings, it trades at least four points below its long-term average.

Now, Elliott sees a path to long-term double-digit earnings per share growth, which could benefit investors through higher earnings growth and a higher valuation. In the near-term, it sees upside of at least 50%, something I entirely agree on.

Even better, because of its diversified business model, PepsiCo has always been one of my favorite consumer staples and a prime stock for a retirement account.

That’s also why I believe we could see consistent dividend growth in the 6-8% range.

To me, PepsiCo is now a Strong Buy.

Putting everything together, these four picks have an average yield of 5.6%, a fantastic number in light of growth potential and safety.

I look forward to your view on this, and I can tell you I will continue to build on this portfolio, as I have a lot more ideas I didn’t mention in this article.

For now, this is my takeaway:

Takeaway

I’m never retiring. At least, if it’s up to me.

However, that doesn’t mean focusing on retirement investing isn’t super important, especially as we are dealing with a wide range of risks like "higher for longer" inflation, the Silver Tsunami creating Social Security headwinds, and massive AI disruption.

That’s why I’m better safe than sorry when it comes to preparing for retirement.

In this article, I discussed four stocks I would make the core of my retirement portfolio, as they deliver an average yield of slightly more than 5.5%, healthy payout ratios, solid balance sheets, and business models that should provide me with inflation-beating dividend growth.

In the weeks and months ahead, I’ll provide more ideas and look for ways to incorporate more growth in a retirement portfolio, a subject I believe is often neglected.

data.title: BYD Shares Priced Attractively For Long-Term Investors After Sale Of Berkshire Hathaway Stake
data.description:
More than a few hopeful investors tend to follow Berkshire Hathaway’s quarterly disclosures as a guide to which equities they should be buying and selling. Buffettbuys.com is one of several websites that track the Oracle of Omaha’s moves.

Much of the time, Buffett watchers do well. Over the past five years Berkshire stock has returned a compound annual growth rate (including dividend reinvestment) of 14.3%, not far behind the 16% CAGR returned over the same period by the S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY), a market benchmark.

Why, therefore, might the shares of BYD Company Ltd. (OTCPK:BYDDY)(OTCPK:BYDDF), the Chinese maker of batteries and BEVs, currently rate a BUY rather than a sell? After all, didn’t Berkshire Hathaway recently dispose of its remaining BYD equity stake, albeit at a rather handsome profit?

CEO Warren Buffett, urged on by the late Charlie Munger, another of the company’s top executives, took a chance on BYD in 2008, when the company was much smaller and little known in the West. Over the past 17 years, BYD has grown into one of China’s most prominent enterprises, a leading and acclaimed manufacturer and seller of BEVs and batteries not only in its home market but also in Europe and other global markets.

In August 2022, per CNBC, Berkshire began selling the 225 million share position it had bought in 2008 for $230 million. At one point, the company’s BYD position was worth $9 billion. With about a quarter of its original BYD stake remaining last year, Berkshire’s position amounted to less than 5% of the automaker’s outstanding.

Buffett appeared on CNBC in 2023, explaining that BYD remained an extraordinary company run by an extraordinary person – the founder, Wang Chuanfu – but “I think that we’ll find things to do with the money that I’ll feel better about.” With the investing discipline that has made Buffett famous, he and his colleagues evidently decided to lock in the tremendous profit from their BYD stake and search for the next opportunity, which in no way should be seen as casting the Chinese automaker in a negative light.

In addition to that explicit explanation, several more are plausible. One is geopolitical risk, BYD is in the process of competing successfully against the West’s incumbent auto industry; several names such as Ford Motor Co. (F), Stellantis N.V. (STLA) and Nissan Motor Co. (OTCPK:NSANY) are losing European market share and scrambling to develop and manufacture models that can contend with the latest from China. The threat to West’s automakers as employers and economic mainstays have increased diplomatic and trade tensions with China; repercussions could result.

For the time being BYD – which sells only battery electric vehicles (BEVs) – and other Chinese brands are largely shut out of North America by 100% import tariffs. What will happen when the first pure Chinese brand decides to build in the U.S.? (Volvo, which maintains its Swedish brand character, is owned by Chinese automaker Geely and currently manufactures in South Carolina.) The threat to Ford, Stellantis and General Motors Co. is obvious and growing.

Based on enthusiastic reception of BYD BEV models like the Atto 3, currently making inroads from the U.K to Bangladesh, U.S. consumers eventually will provide a lucrative potential market. With over a million Atto 3s sold worldwide, the model serves as a linchpin of BYD’s global strategy. Prices start at the equivalent of $16,000 in China, $44,000 in Europe; reviews give Atto 3 high marks for quality, comfort and ease of charging.

BEV share of the U.S. automotive market, currently at 7.4%, is likely to continue rising, albeit more slowly than once projected in light of the end of government incentives. As more U.S. consumers consider BEVs, the immense cost and price advantage of BYD models – and those of other Chinese BEV brands – are likely to win it a share of the market.

Ten years ago, BYD’s annual sales stood at $12.74 billion; in 2024 they reached $108.3 billion, an average compound annual growth rate of 27.4%. In the latest reported quarter sales were $27.7 billion, still an impressive increase of 13.4% over the previous year’s second quarter. Pre-tax profit for 2024 was $6.9 billion, representing a compound annual growth rate for the previous decade of 28.6%.

As impressive as BYD’s growth and financial performance have been the automaker faces short-term obstacles posed by the nearly one hundred Chinese carmakers and the discounting of prices that has resulted as companies attempt to stave off consolidation, mergers and closures. Earlier this month, BYD cut its sales target for this year by 16% to 4.6 million vehicles from the 5.5 million vehicles it forecast in March.

A BYD executive predicted a shakeout for the Chinese auto industry as Beijing continues a crackdown on discounting among automakers.

“I think our profit will still remain strong,” said Stella Li, executive vice president of BYD. “I think you will see more Chinese companies come overseas, but the overseas market is not that simple.”

The investment thesis for BYD is simple: the company has lots of room to run in Europe, the Middle East, South America, and Southeast Asia, with North America as virgin territory. The automaker appears to be the leader in a very crowded pack of Chinese competitors that is destined to be culled, with only the very fittest surviving as independents.

As Berkshire Hathaway likely understood, the geopolitical risk is considerable. The U.S. and European Union aren’t likely to allow BYD simply to march over their incumbent auto industries without pushback. For now the pushback takes the form of tariffs. Eventually, one of the Detroit Three may be “encouraged” to create a joint manufacturing venture on U.S. soil with BYD or another large Chinese automaker. Or BYD may take the bold step of building a U.S. manufacturing plant of its own.

BYD appears well positioned to become a much better-known brand in the West and eventually in the U.S. As such, its current price may be seen one day as big an opportunity as the price was in 2008 for Berkshire Hathaway. Thus, I rate the stock a BUY for long-term investors.

BYDDY shares are the ADRs for ordinary BYD shares; BYDDF represents the H shares that trade in Hong Kong, typically at close to the same price as BYDDY shares.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: 51 September S&P Hi-Yield Aristocrats: 5 To Buy
data.description:
Foreword

While over half of this collection of The S&P Hi-Yield Aristocrats is too pricey, or reveal somewhat skinny-dividends, nine of the ten lowest priced Dogs of the Hi-Yield Aristocrats were ready to buy. September found Flowers Foods (FLO), Robert Half (RHI), Verizon (VZ), Amcor (AMCR), Edison (EIX), NNN REIT (NNN), Perrigo (PRGO), Avista (AVA), and Franklin (BEN) in the top ten lived up to the dogcatcher ideal of annual dividends from $1K invested exceeding single share price. Only Realty Income (O) was too over-priced to make that list.

With renewed downside market pressure to 4.5%, it would be possible for the missing Realty Income stock of the top-yield Aristocrats dogs to become elite fair-priced with its annual yield (from $1K invested) meeting or exceeding their single share prices.

[See a summary of top ten fair-priced September HI-Yield Aristocrat Dogs (below actionable conclusion #21 mid-article) and the “safer” choice and tag-alongs found by digging deeper into the Afterword, at the bottom of this article.]

Actionable Conclusions (1-10): Brokers Expect 21.59% To 66.98% Net Gains From Top-Ten Hi-Yield Aristocrat Dogs By September 2026

Five of ten top Hi-Yield Aristocrats (tinted gray in the chart below) were among the top ten price gainers for the coming year based on analyst 1-year target prices. So, this September 2025 yield-based forecast for them, as graded by Wall St. wizard estimates, was 50% accurate.

Estimated dividend-returns from $1000 invested in the ten highest-yielding stocks and their aggregate one-year analyst median target prices, as reported by YCharts, created the 2025-26 data points below. (Note: one-year target-prices estimated by single analysts were not applied.) Ten probable profit-generating trades projected to September, 2026 were:

Perrigo was projected to net $669.77 based on the median of target prices estimated by 5 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 52% less than the market as a whole.

Amcor PLC was projected to net $368.24, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 11 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 28% under the market as a whole.

Robert Half was projected to net $353.13, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 9 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 3% more than the market as a whole.

Kenvue (KVUE) was projected to net $321.79, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 17 analysts, less broker fees. A Beta numbers was not available for KVUE.

Sonoco Products (SON) was projected to net $294.12 based on the median of target price estimates from 10 analysts, plus dividends, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 30% under the market as a whole.

UGI Inc (UGI) was projected to net $279.44, based on dividends, plus median target price estimates from 4 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 26% under the market as a whole.

Flowers Foods was projected to net $265.99, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 7 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 71% under the market as a whole.

Edison International was projected to net $264.12, based on the median of target price estimates from 13 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 19% less than the market as a whole.

Target (TGT) was projected to net $224.46, based on the median of target price estimates from 36 analysts, plus the estimated annual dividend, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 16% greater than the market as a whole.

Hormel Foods (HRL) was projected to net $215.86, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 10 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 69% below the market as a whole.

The average net gain in dividend and price was estimated at 32.57% on $10k invested as $1k in each of these top ten Hi-Yield Aristocrat stocks. This gain estimate was subject to average risk/volatility 31% less than the market as a whole.

The Dividend Dogs Rule

Stocks earned the "dog" moniker by exhibiting three traits: (1) paying reliable, repeating dividends, (2) their prices fell to where (3) yield (dividend/price) grew higher than their peers. Thus, the highest yielding stocks in any collection became known as "dogs." More precisely, these are, in fact, best called, "underdogs".

September 2025 Hi-Yield Aristocrats By Total Annual Returns

September 2025 Hi-Yield Aristocrats By Analyst Targets

September 2025 Hi-Yield Aristocrats By Yield

Actionable Conclusions (11-20): 10 Top September Hi-Yield Aristocrat Yields Ranged 5.33% To 7.57% Per YCharts

Top ten Hi-Yield Aristocrats by yield represented eight of eleven Morningstar sectors.

In first place, was the lone consumer staples top ten Hi-Yield Aristocrat, Flowers Foods [1]. Then, one industrials representative placed second, Robert Half [2].

The lone communication services member placed third, Verizon [3]. There followed the materials sector member in fourth place, Amcor [4].

Two Hi-Yield Aristocrat utilities representatives placed fifth and ninth,

Edison [5], and Avista [9].

Two real estate sector dogs took the sixth, and eighth positions: NNN REIT [6], and Realty Income [8]. Placing seventh was a health care member, Perrigo [7].

Finally, one financials stalwart, placed tenth, Franklin Resources [10], to complete the September Dogs of the Hi-Yield Aristocrats by yield.

The Top 15 of 51 September 2025 Hi-Yield Aristocrats By Alphabetical Tickers

Actionable Conclusions (21-35): 15 Top September Hi-Yield S&P Aristocrat Stocks By Yield Ticker Show Only Edison placed in the top 15 by Yield, Target, and Return

All 51 Hi-Yield Aristo Dogs tell their tale.

Dividend Vs. Price Results

The top graph above shows relative strengths of the top ten September Hi-Yield Aristocrats by dividend and price as of market close 9/23/2025.

This month nine of the top-ten Hi Yield S&P Aristocrats by yield show ideal status because their prices are below the projected annual dividends from $1k invested in each.

S&P Hi-Yield Aristocrats Costs per Dividend Dollar as of 9/23/25

The lower chart above reveals all fifty-one Hi-Yield Aristocrat dogs subjected to the same test: divide the price of each stock by the annual dividend. Thus is calculated the cost per dividend dollar for each stock.

The least expensive dog of the Hi-Yield Aristocrats is Flowers at $13.21 per dividend dollar and the most expensive is Coca-Cola (KO) at $32.70 per dividend dollar.

Actionable Conclusion (21): Twenty-five of Fifty-One Aristocrats Show Negative Free Cash Flow Margins

A negative gap between dividend yield and free cash flow yield defines a cash-poor stock. That is, their dividend payout exceeds their cash on hand to pay dividends. Here is where twenty-five of the fifty-one hi-dividend payers fail to advance as “safer” dogs. They are considered unsafe to buy with their dividend yields exceeding free cash flow yields.

Remember this dogcatcher yield-based stock-picking strategy is contrarian. That means rooting for (buying) the underdog is productive when you don't already own those stocks. If you do hold those stocks, then you must look for opportune pull-backs in price to add to your position to best improve your dividend yield. Plenty of pull-back opportunities appear to be ahead. Dread the Fed, uncertainty, deals, wars, and tariffs, too.

Price Drops or Dividend Increases Could Get All Ten Top Yield Aristocrats Back to "Fair Price" Rates For Investors

The charts above retain the current dividend amount and adjust share price to produce a yield (from $1K invested) to equal or exceed the single share price of each stock. As the top illustration shows, nine in the top ten are already ideally priced. The laggard is within $3 of getting there.

Two alternatives, of course, could be that this one company raises dividends, or split shares. Those choices may be too risky in these highly disrupted, inflationary, recessionary, yet cash-rich, and volatile trade-warfare times. Mr. Market must be far more effective at moving prices up or down to appropriate amounts, just watch and buy when your targeted stock price moves to a sweet spot.

Actionable Conclusions: (22-31) Broker 1-Yr. Targets Showed 17.84% To 62.48% Top Ten Aristocrat Upsides To September, 2026; (32). One -11.25% Downsider Trailed

To quantify top-dog rankings, analyst median price-target estimates provided a "market-sentiment" gauge of upside-potential. Added to the simple high-yield "dog" metrics, analyst median-price target-estimates provided another tool to dig-out bargains.

Analysts Forecast A 37.04% Advantage For 5 Highest-Yield, Lowest-Priced of 10 Aristocrats As of September 23, 2026

Ten-top Aristocrats were culled by yield for this monthly-update. Yield (dividend / price) results as verified by YCharts did the ranking.

As noted-above, top-ten S&P Hi-Yield Aristocrats selected 9/23/25 by yield, represented eight of eleven sectors. Consumer Discretionary, Energy, and Technology missed the top-ten cut.

Actionable Conclusions: Analysts Expected 5 Lowest-Priced of Ten S&P Highest-Yield Aristocrats (34) To Deliver 34.9% Vs. (35) 25.47% Net Gains by All Ten Come September 23, 2026

$5000 invested as $1k in each of the five lowest-priced stocks in the top ten Dividend Aristocrats by-yield were predicted by analyst 1-year targets to deliver 17.40% more gain than from $5,000 invested in all-ten. The very lowest-priced top ten yielding stock, Amcor, showed top analyst-estimated gains of 32.06%.

The five lowest-priced top-yield S&P Hi-Yield Aristocrat dogs for September were: Amcor; Flowers; Perrigo; Franklin; Robert Half, with prices ranging from $8.09 to $33.79.

Five higher-priced Hi-Yield S&P Aristocrat top-yield dogs as of September 23 were: Avista; NNN REIT; Verizon; Edison; Realty Income, with prices ranging from $36.76 to $59.55.

The distinction between five low-priced dividend dogs and the general field of ten reflected Michael B. O'Higgins' "basic method" for beating the Dow. The scale of projected gains based on analyst targets added a unique element of "market sentiment" gauging upside potential. It provided a here-and-now equivalent of waiting a year to find out what might happen in the market.

Caution is advised, however, since analysts are historically only 15% to 85% accurate on the direction of change and just 0% to 15% accurate on the degree of change.

As summer euphoria dies and the feckless Fed-relieved slide and dithering presidential proclamations, deals and threats gain momentum into the fall of 2025, with the exception of the few hot ticket stocks, look for all of the fifteen top S&P Hi-Yield Aristocrats to become Fair-priced… slowly, but surely, but perhaps, quite suddenly.

Afterword

September Dogs of the S&P Hi-Yield Aristocrats

Mid-September 2025, 26 of the 51Hi-Yield Aristocrats were tagged "safer" for dividends as they showed positive one-year returns and free-cash-flow-yields greater-than dividend-yields. YCharts 9/23/25 data showed five fair-priced, Verizon, NNN REIT, Kenvue, UGI, and United Bankshares [W.Va] (UBSI).

How Aristocrat 'Safer' Dividend Vs. Price Results Produced A "Fair Price” Result For Investors

The chart at the top above reveals dividends from $1K invested in five of our top 'safer' dividend Aristocrats conforms to the dogcatcher standard of being equal to (or greater than) the price of a single share. The middle chart shows the recent prices and the bottom chart adjusts that share price to produce a yield (from $1K invested) equal to or greater than the single share price of each stock; a so-called, “fair price.”

A market correction of only 47.5% could put all ten share prices of these “safer” survivors above or equal to their dividend payouts from $1k invested.

There are, of course, other ways for dividends from $1K invested to exceed the price of a single share of stock. That would be for the boards of directors these companies to raise dividends or split shares to the dogcatcher ideal level. That’s a lot to ask with all the corporate funds doing so much “good” elsewhere.

Stay tuned for future Dividend Dogcatcher S&P Hi-Yield Aristocrat updates, out here, in public, away from the pay-walled Seeking Alpha Investing Group site (formerly known as the Marketplace).

Net gain estimates mentioned above did not factor-in any foreign or domestic tax problems resulting from distributions. Consult your tax advisor regarding the source and consequences of "dividends" from any investment.

Stocks listed above were suggested only as possible starting points for your "Safer" Aristocratic dividend stock research process. These were not recommendations.

Disclaimer: This article is for informational and educational purposes only and should not be construed to constitute investment advice. Nothing contained herein shall constitute a solicitation, recommendation or endorsement to buy or sell any security. Prices and returns on equities in this article except as noted are listed without consideration of fees, commissions, taxes, penalties, or interest payable due to purchasing, holding, or selling same.

Graphs and charts were compiled by Rydlun & Co., LLC from data derived from Indexarb; YCharts; finance.yahoo.com; analyst mean target price by YCharts. Open source dog art from dividenddogcatcher.com.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Tariffs Hit Pharma
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Trump slaps 100% tariffs on drugs, 25% on heavy trucks and more. (00:28) Federal agencies told to prepare for mass firings if government shuts down. (01:21) Upward pressure on Fed's preferred inflation gauge — PCE price index — is seen persisting in August. (03:05)

This is an abridged transcript.

President Trump announced sweeping new tariffs, including 100% on branded and patented pharmaceutical drugs, to take effect on October 1.

A company is exempted if it's actively building a new manufacturing facility in the U.S.

Trump wrote in a Truth Social post, ‘IS BUILDING’ will be defined as, ‘breaking ground’ and/or ‘under construction.’ He goes on to say, “There will, therefore, be no Tariff on these Pharmaceutical Products if construction has started.”

Among other levies are 50% on kitchen cabinets and bathroom vanities, 30% on upholstered furniture, and 25% on heavy trucks.

The president wrote on Truth Social, “We need our Truckers to be financially healthy and strong, for many reasons, but above all else, for National Security purposes!”

The White House has directed federal agencies to prepare for mass firings if the government shuts down on October 1, targeting programs whose funding will expire by the deadline and have no alternative funding source.

The Office of Management and Budget also instructed agencies to prepare reduction-in-force notices for employees in programs that are not consistent with President Donald Trump's priorities.

"RIF notices will be in addition to any furlough notices provided due to the lapse in appropriation," OMB wrote in a memo, first reported by Politico.

"Once FY 2026 appropriations are enacted, agencies should revise their RIFs as needed to retain the minimal number of employees necessary to carry out statutory functions," it added.

OMB noted that if Congress successfully passes a "clean" continuing resolution to fund the government before the deadline, the steps in the memo won't be necessary.

In the past, federal workers were furloughed during government shutdowns till a budget deal was reached, after which they'd return to work.

"This is an attempt at intimidation," Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer (D-NY) said about OMB's directive.

Federal funding is set to end on September 30. The Republican-controlled House passed a stopgap measure to fund federal operations through November 21 without Democratic support.

The Senate needs at least seven Democratic votes to pass the measure. Democrats' demands for the funding bill include an extension of Affordable Care Act subsidies.

Economists don't expect any big swings when the Commerce Department's Bureau of Economic Analysis releases the August Personal Income and Outlays report today.

Federal Reserve officials will be keyed in on the core personal consumption expenditures price index, what they consider a good measure of underlying inflation trends.

The core PCE price index excludes food and energy prices, which can swing from month to month. For August, the consensus of economists expects a 2.9% Y/Y increase in core PCE, unchanged from July. Meanwhile, the consensus projects a 0.2% M/M rise vs. +0.3% in the prior month.

Including food and energy prices, the PCE price index is expected to increase 2.7% Y/Y, ticking up from the 2.6% Y/Y rise in July. Month over month, it's expected to grow 0.3%, up from 0.2% in the previous month.

Another part of the Personal Income and Outlays report is consumer spending. That's expected to rise 0.4% M/M, slowing from the 0.5% increase in July. At the same time, personal income is projected to increase 0.3% M/M, down from +0.4% in the prior month, indicating that consumer spending growth continues to outpace income growth.

What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha:

Trump presses Japan, South Korea to pay billions 'upfront' in investments

Italy's antitrust fines oil companies Eni, Esso, Q8 and others €937M for fuel market collusion

Costco plans 35 new warehouses in fiscal 2026 as member upgrades and digital sales accelerate

Dow, S&P and Nasdaq futures are in the green. Crude oil is down 0.2% at $64/barrel. Bitcoin is up 0.6% at $109,000. Gold is flat at $3,751.

The FTSE 100 is up 0.4% and the DAX is up 0.3%.

The biggest movers for the day premarket: Intel (NASDAQ:INTC) +4%, GlobalFoundries (NASDAQ:GFS) +4% - Chipmakers rallied after the WSJ reported the Trump administration is weighing a policy requiring U.S. firms to manufacture as many chips domestically as they import. Companies falling short of the 1:1 ratio could face tariffs, the report said.

On today’s economic calendar:

  • 8:30 am Personal Income and Outlays

  • 9:00 am Richmond Federal Reserve Bank President Thomas Barkin will speak before the Peterson Institute for International Economics.

  • 10:00 am Consumer Sentiment

  • 1:00 pm Federal Reserve Vice Chair for Supervision Michelle Bowman will participate in "Approach to Monetary Policy Decision-Making" conversation hosted by the Forecasters' Club of New York.

And finally it’s quiz day. Seeking Alpha’s weekly news quiz is live. One of the questions says: Barnes & Noble continued on its return to a growth phase with a bid submitted to pick up the assets of which bankrupt chain? All questions are multiple choice. I’ll leave a link to the quiz in the show notes section.
